texto: Just like a constant diet of fast food makes us flabby, so too a constant intake of social media to the neglect of books and thoughtful meditation will make our brains flabby. So if you find it difficult to read more than five pages at a time, or you find yourself falling asleep as soon as you crack open a book, that is a sign that you should be putting in the work that it takes to be an active reader.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Aimee Byrd: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de passagem do tempo e prioridades. O trecho “Just like a constant diet of fast food makes us flabby, so too a constant intake of social media …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, reading-books, constant pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de dia-chuvoso e trabalho.

texto: Good books last longer than blog posts, which fade into cyberspace, hoping that a Google search will bring them to light again one day. And they shape a higher purpose in shaping a reader....Read books!

texto: Who is willing to suffer for a Savior they won't even trouble themselves to learn about?
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Aimee Byrd que provoca temas ligados a sabedoria. Ao questionar “Who is willing to suffer for a Savior they won't even trouble themselves to learn about?”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre sabedoria, christianity, faith, com foco em faith e perseverance.
